content.text: What it is

The test pyramid for agentic AI applications is a layered testing strategy that allocates test effort across four distinct tiers: fast deterministic unit tests at the base, integration tests that exercise real LLM calls with stubbed external dependencies, LLM-as-judge evaluation suites that score output quality and safety, and a small set of end-to-end tests that validate complete user journeys. The shape of the pyramid — many tests at the bottom, few at the top — encodes the economic and speed trade-offs unique to AI systems, where a single E2E test may cost dollars in API fees and take minutes to execute.
Why it matters

Traditional software testing pyramids focus on three layers: unit, integration, and E2E. Agentic AI adds a fourth layer — evals — that has no direct equivalent in deterministic software testing. Without an explicit place in the pyramid, evals tend to be run ad hoc by ML engineers, disconnected from the CI/CD pipeline and invisible to software engineers who manage the deployment cycle. Defining where evals sit in the pyramid — above integration tests, below E2E — gives teams a shared mental model for how to allocate test infrastructure budget and where to investigate when a deployment gate fails.
Benefits
Explicit cost allocation: placing expensive LLM-call tests in the eval and E2E tiers prevents them from inflating the CI cost of every pull request.
Faster feedback loops: the majority of bugs are caught by cheap unit tests before the code ever makes an LLM API call.
Shared vocabulary: engineers, ML researchers, and QA all use the same four-layer model when discussing what broke and where.
Clear ownership: each pyramid layer maps to a team — unit and integration to engineering, evals to the ML platform team, E2E to QA.
Independent scaling: eval infrastructure (GPU-backed LLM judges) can be scaled and budgeted separately from unit test runners.
Trade-offs and limitations
The base layer is weaker for AI: unit tests cannot verify the quality of an LLM's output, only the correctness of the code wrapping it.
Eval results are probabilistic: running the same eval dataset twice may produce slightly different scores due to LLM sampling temperature.
No layer fully validates emergent multi-step behaviors: complex agent trajectories through multiple tools are hard to cover at any individual tier.
Eval layer requires curated datasets: the quality of the eval tier depends entirely on the quality of the evaluation dataset, which requires ongoing human curation.
How it works
The
unit layer

covers all deterministic logic: prompt template rendering, tool selection routing, JSON parsing of LLM outputs, retry logic, error handling, and flag evaluation. LLM responses are mocked using fixtures, so tests run in milliseconds and produce deterministic results. The goal is 100% branch coverage of all code paths that do not depend on model output quality.
The
integration layer

makes real LLM API calls against a specific model version but stubs or sandboxes external systems (databases, APIs, email servers). These tests verify that the agent actually calls the LLM correctly, that the real model output can be parsed by the agent's output parser, and that the control flow between planner, tools, and memory works end-to-end for typical input scenarios. Run on every PR, scoped to the most important happy-path scenarios.
The
eval layer

runs a curated dataset of (input, expected output) pairs through the agent and scores the outputs using an LLM-as-judge or rule-based scorer. This layer catches quality regressions: the agent gives a factually wrong answer, the tone is off, or a safety constraint is violated. Run on a schedule (nightly or pre-release), not on every PR, because of cost and run time.
The
E2E layer

executes complete user journeys against a staging environment with real external dependencies. It validates that the full system — LLM, tools, memory, frontend, backend — works together for the scenarios that matter most to the business. Run pre-release, not in CI, and scoped to five to ten representative journeys.
Architecture

attrs.alt: Test pyramid for agentic AI showing unit, integration, eval, and E2E layers with relative proportions

content.text: The pyramid's shape reflects both count and cost. Unit tests number in the hundreds; E2E tests number in the tens. The eval layer is unique to AI systems: it sits above integration tests in both cost and non-determinism, but below E2E tests in scope. This placement ensures that expensive LLM judge calls are reserved for pre-release gates, not per-PR CI runs.
When to use it

Apply the four-layer pyramid for any production agentic application that involves at least one LLM call in the critical path. For internal prototypes or developer tools with a small user base, the integration and eval layers can be merged into a single stage run on a daily schedule. The full four-layer model pays off most when multiple engineers contribute to the same agent codebase and quality regressions are difficult to attribute to specific changes.
How to implement
Set up a unit test suite using pytest (Python) or Jest (TypeScript) with LLM response fixtures; target 80%+ code coverage of non-LLM logic.
Write integration tests that make real API calls to the LLM provider using a low-cost test model (e.g., Haiku instead of Sonnet) with VCR cassettes for reproducibility.
Build an eval dataset of 50–200 (input, expected criteria) pairs; write a scoring function that accepts agent output and returns a pass/fail or 0–1 score.
Integrate unit tests into the PR CI gate; schedule integration tests as a post-merge check; run evals nightly and on release-branch creation.
Write E2E tests using a tool like Playwright or a custom test harness against a staging environment; execute them as the final gate before production release.
Track eval score trends over time in a dashboard so regressions are visible before a release decision is made.
Examples

# Layer 1: Unit test — LLM response is mocked
def test_tool_router_selects_web_search_for_factual_query(mocker):
    mock_llm = mocker.patch("agent.llm.call")
    mock_llm.return_value = '{"tool": "web_search", "query": "GDP of France 2024"}'
    result = route_tool("What is the GDP of France in 2024?")
    assert result.tool == "web_search"
    assert "GDP" in result.query

# Layer 2: Integration test — real LLM call, external APIs stubbed
@pytest.mark.integration
def test_agent_completes_factual_lookup(vcr_cassette):
    # vcr_cassette records/replays the LLM HTTP call for determinism
    with vcr_cassette("factual_lookup.yaml"):
        response = agent.run("What is the Eiffel Tower's height in meters?")
    assert "330" in response.text or "324" in response.text

# Layer 3: Eval — LLM-as-judge scores quality
@pytest.mark.eval
def test_agent_eval_factual_accuracy(eval_dataset, judge_llm):
    scores = []
    for item in eval_dataset["factual"]:
        response = agent.run(item["input"])
        score = judge_llm.score(
            response=response.text,
            criteria=item["criteria"],
        )
        scores.append(score)
    avg = sum(scores) / len(scores)
    assert avg >= 0.85, f"Eval accuracy {avg:.2f} below threshold 0.85"
Real-world example

A team building a research assistant agent structured their test suite as follows: 340 unit tests covering prompt template rendering, JSON parsing, and tool routing ran in 8 seconds on every PR. 18 integration tests with VCR cassettes for LLM calls ran in 90 seconds as a post-merge check. A nightly eval pipeline ran 120 question-answer pairs through the agent and scored them using GPT-4 as a judge, tracking factual accuracy and citation quality. Six E2E tests in Playwright validated the complete user flow — from query submission to rendered response with citations — against the staging environment before each weekly release.

When a new prompt change caused a 7% drop in factual accuracy, the eval pipeline caught it the night after merge. The team reverted the prompt change and the E2E tests for that release passed without requiring a manual QA cycle on the broken behavior. Without the eval layer, the regression would have reached production users.
Common mistakes
Running evals on every PR: the cost and run time of eval suites make them unsuitable as per-PR gates; run them on a schedule or on release branches.
Treating E2E tests as the primary quality gate: relying on E2E tests to catch regressions means bugs are found slowly and expensively.
No mocking at the unit layer: unit tests that make real LLM calls are integration tests disguised as unit tests — they are slow, flaky, and cost money on every run.
Using a fixed threshold without tracking trends: an eval score of 0.85 may be acceptable today but was 0.92 last month — tracking trends catches slow-burn regressions.
Skipping the eval layer entirely: pure unit and integration tests cannot validate whether the agent's answers are actually correct or safe.
Best practices
Use pytest markers (
@pytest.mark.unit
@pytest.mark.integration
@pytest.mark.eval
) to group tests by layer and run each layer on a separate CI schedule.
Keep integration tests reproducible with VCR cassettes or a deterministic test model configuration (temperature=0, seed=42) so they do not produce flaky results.
Track eval score trends in a time-series database and alert when the 7-day moving average drops more than 3 percentage points.
Budget the eval layer explicitly: know the cost per eval run and set a monthly cap so eval infrastructure cost does not surprise the finance team.
Rotate the eval judge model separately from the agent model so judge drift does not mask agent regressions.
Performance and scalability

The performance of the overall test suite is determined by how well each layer respects its speed and cost budget. Unit tests should run in under 60 seconds for 500 tests; integration tests with VCR cassettes in under 3 minutes for 50 tests; eval suites in under 30 minutes for 200 cases using parallelized LLM calls (5–10 concurrent); E2E tests in under 20 minutes for 10 scenarios. These targets allow the full CI pipeline including unit and integration tests to complete in under 10 minutes, while the heavier eval and E2E tiers run asynchronously without blocking developer throughput.

promptfoo, braintrust

Playwright, Cypress
Comparison with alternatives
Strategy
AI quality coverage
CI cost
Four-layer AI pyramid
High (eval layer catches quality drift)
Medium (evals on schedule, not per PR)
Unit + E2E only
Low (misses quality regressions)
High (E2E is expensive per run)
Manual QA only
Variable (human judgment)
High (engineering-time cost)
Security and reliability

The eval layer is a potential security blind spot: if the eval dataset does not include adversarial inputs (prompt injections, jailbreak attempts, personally identifiable information leakage scenarios), the eval suite will score high while the agent remains vulnerable to these attacks. Add a security eval partition that covers known attack patterns and score it separately from quality evals so security regressions are visible independently of accuracy changes. Store VCR cassettes and eval datasets in version-controlled repositories with appropriate access controls — they contain model inputs and outputs that may be sensitive.
Structuring the unit layer for AI agents

The unit layer for an agentic AI application covers all code that does not depend on a model's probabilistic output. In practice, this is a larger surface than many teams initially expect. It includes:

Prompt template rendering: given a set of context variables, does the template produce the expected string? These tests catch accidentally deleted placeholders, incorrect JSON serialization of tool definitions, and context window overflow checks.

Output parser logic: does the structured output parser correctly handle both valid and malformed model responses? Mock the LLM with a pre-written fixture that simulates a malformed JSON response and verify the error path.
Tool selection routing: given a mocked LLM response indicating a specific tool choice, does the router instantiate and call the correct tool with the correct parameters?
Retry and backoff logic: does the agent correctly retry on a 429 rate limit response after the expected delay? Does it give up after N attempts?
Memory read and write: does the memory module correctly serialize and deserialize conversation history? Does it correctly truncate when the context limit is reached?
Feature flag evaluation: does the agent select the correct code path for a given flag state? Test both the ON and OFF paths.

# Unit test: prompt template rendering with context overflow guard
import pytest
from agent.prompts import SystemPromptTemplate

def test_system_prompt_renders_tool_definitions():
    tools = [{"name": "web_search", "description": "Search the web"}]
    prompt = SystemPromptTemplate(tools=tools, persona="helpful assistant")
    rendered = prompt.render()
    assert "web_search" in rendered
    assert "helpful assistant" in rendered

def test_system_prompt_raises_when_tools_exceed_token_budget():
    # Generate 200 tools to overflow the budget
    tools = [{"name": f"tool_{i}", "description": "x" * 500} for i in range(200)]
    with pytest.raises(ValueError, match="exceeds context budget"):
        SystemPromptTemplate(tools=tools, persona="test").render()

def test_output_parser_handles_malformed_json():
    from agent.parser import ToolCallParser
    parser = ToolCallParser()
    # Model occasionally returns markdown fences around JSON
    raw = '```json\n{"tool": "web_search", "query": "test"}\n```'
    result = parser.parse(raw)
    assert result.tool == "web_search"
    assert result.query == "test"
Building the integration layer with VCR cassettes

VCR (video cassette recorder) testing records the full HTTP request-response cycle of a real LLM API call and saves it to a YAML fixture file. On subsequent test runs, the HTTP call is intercepted and the recorded response is returned without hitting the real API. This gives integration tests the realism of real LLM responses without the cost, latency, or non-determinism of live API calls.

Cassettes should be committed to version control alongside the tests that use them. When the agent's prompt changes significantly — enough that the model's response to the same input would change meaningfully — delete the relevant cassettes and record new ones using a dedicated recording job in CI. This recreation step is a useful forcing function: it makes prompt changes explicit in the pull request, because the cassette diff shows exactly what the model's response changed to.
Designing the eval layer scoring function
The eval layer's scoring function determines whether an agent output meets quality criteria. Two patterns are common:

Rule-based scoring: the scorer checks for specific strings, JSON schema compliance, or regex matches. Fast, cheap, fully deterministic. Suitable for structural requirements ("the response must contain a citation", "the response must not contain the customer's full name").

LLM-as-judge scoring: a second LLM call evaluates the quality of the first LLM's output using a rubric. More expensive and slightly non-deterministic, but can assess semantic correctness, tone, and nuanced safety properties that rules cannot capture.
Key takeaways
The agentic AI test pyramid has four layers: unit (70%), integration (20%), eval (7%), E2E (3%) by test count.
The eval layer is unique to AI systems and sits above integration tests in cost and non-determinism but below E2E in scope.
Unit tests mock LLM responses and run on every PR; evals use real LLM calls and run on a schedule to control cost.
Track eval score trends over time — a slowly declining average is a regression even if no single run crosses the threshold.
The E2E layer validates complete user journeys in staging and should be scoped to the five to ten most business-critical scenarios.
Add a security eval partition covering adversarial inputs; do not rely on quality evals to catch safety regressions.
